articles.headline: Prime Minister Inaugurates First Major Industrial Cluster Under National Green Hydrogen Mission
articles.explainer:
• The Prime Minister today inaugurated India's first dedicated Green Hydrogen Industrial Cluster in Gujarat, marking a significant step towards energy independence and decarbonization.
• The cluster, developed with public-private partnerships, will focus on large-scale production, storage, and export of green hydrogen and its derivatives.
• It is expected to create thousands of jobs, attract substantial foreign investment, and reduce India's reliance on fossil fuels.
• This initiative aligns with India's ambitious climate targets and commitment to achieving Net Zero emissions by 2070.
• Advanced electrolyser technologies and renewable energy integration are key features of the new cluster.
gsSubTopics: Infrastructure: Energy
Investment models
Environmental pollution and degradation
Conservation
Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life
prelimsPoints: National Green Hydrogen Mission launched in 2023.
Target: 5 MMT (Million Metric Tonnes) of green hydrogen production capacity by 2030.
Aim to reduce fossil fuel imports by over ₹1 lakh crore and abate nearly 50 MMT of annual GHG emissions.
Key components: SIGHT (Strategic Interventions for Green Hydrogen Transition) Programme, pilot projects, R&D, skill development.
Gujarat, Karnataka, and Andhra Pradesh are emerging as key hubs for green hydrogen projects.
mainsPoints: Discuss the strategic importance of green hydrogen for India's energy security and geopolitical standing.
Analyze the economic potential of green hydrogen, including job creation, investment attraction, and export opportunities.
Evaluate the challenges in scaling up green hydrogen production, such as cost, infrastructure, and technological advancements.
Examine the environmental benefits of transitioning to green hydrogen in hard-to-abate sectors like steel, cement, and fertilizers.
Suggest policy measures and international collaborations necessary to accelerate India's green hydrogen ecosystem.
articles.imageDescription: Prime Minister inaugurating a large-scale green hydrogen production facility with solar panels and industrial infrastructure in the background.
articles.trivia: Hydrogen is the most abundant chemical substance in the universe, constituting roughly 75% of all baryonic mass.

prelimsSyllabus.subject: Economic and Social Development
prelimsSyllabus.area: Sustainable Development, Poverty, Inclusion, Demographics, Social Sector Initiatives

articles.headline: Supreme Court Delivers Landmark Verdict on 'Right to be Forgotten' in Digital Age
articles.explainer:
• The Supreme Court today delivered a significant judgment clarifying the scope and applicability of the 'Right to be Forgotten' in India, especially concerning online personal data.
• The ruling emphasizes a delicate balance between an individual's right to privacy and the public's right to information, alongside freedom of speech and expression.
• It mandates specific conditions under which personal information can be de-indexed or removed from public search results and online platforms.
• The verdict provides much-needed clarity for data fiduciaries and individuals regarding data protection and digital rights.
• Experts suggest the ruling will significantly influence future data protection laws and digital governance frameworks in India.
gsSubTopics: Indian Constitution—historical underpinnings, evolution, features, amendments, significant provisions and basic structure.
Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation.

Important aspects of governance, transparency and accountability, e-governance- applications, models, successes, limitations, and potential; citizens charters, transparency & accountability and institutional and other measures.
prelimsPoints: Right to be Forgotten is part of the broader 'right to privacy' under Article 21 of the Indian Constitution.
Puttaswamy v. Union of India (2017) judgment recognized privacy as a fundamental right.
The Personal Data Protection Bill (now Act) has provisions related to the 'right to erasure' or 'right to be forgotten'.
Globally, GDPR (General Data Protection Regulation) in the EU recognizes this right.
It involves removal of personal information from public visibility, not necessarily permanent deletion from databases.
mainsPoints: Analyze the constitutional basis and jurisprudential evolution of the 'Right to be Forgotten' in India.
Discuss the challenges in implementing the 'Right to be Forgotten' in the context of digital platforms and global internet architecture.
Examine the potential conflict between the 'Right to be Forgotten' and other fundamental rights such as freedom of speech and the right to information.
Critically evaluate the implications of this Supreme Court verdict on data governance, individual privacy, and media freedom in India.
Suggest a balanced approach to uphold privacy rights while ensuring transparency and accountability in the digital sphere.
articles.imageDescription: A stylized image showing a lock icon over digital data, representing data privacy and the 'right to be forgotten'.
articles.trivia: The 'Right to be Forgotten' gained prominence after a 2014 European Court of Justice ruling against Google.

articles.headline: India-EU Free Trade Agreement Negotiations Show Positive Momentum
articles.explainer:
• India and the European Union concluded another round of Free Trade Agreement (FTA) negotiations, reporting significant progress on several key chapters.
• Both sides expressed optimism about reaching a comprehensive and mutually beneficial agreement in the near future.
• Key areas of discussion included market access for goods and services, investment protection, intellectual property rights, and geographical indications.
• An FTA is expected to boost bilateral trade and investment, creating new opportunities for businesses in both regions.
• The agreement aims to reduce tariffs and non-tariff barriers, fostering deeper economic integration between India and the EU.
gsSubTopics: Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests.
Effect of policies and politics of developed and developing countries on India’s interests, Indian diaspora.
prelimsPoints: India-EU FTA negotiations resumed in 2022 after an eight-year hiatus.
The EU is one of India's largest trading partners.
Key Indian exports to EU: engineering goods, pharmaceuticals, chemicals, textiles, agricultural products.
Key EU exports to India: machinery, transport equipment, chemicals, diamonds.
India is also negotiating FTAs with countries like the UK, Canada, and Australia.
mainsPoints: Analyze the strategic importance of a comprehensive FTA between India and the European Union for India's economic growth and global positioning.
Discuss the potential benefits and challenges for various sectors of the Indian economy from an India-EU FTA.
Examine how the FTA could impact India's 'Make in India' initiative and its integration into global supply chains.
Evaluate the role of non-tariff barriers and regulatory convergence in the success of the India-EU FTA.
Suggest measures India should take to maximize gains and mitigate risks from such a trade agreement.
articles.imageDescription: Flags of India and the European Union crossed, symbolizing trade and cooperation.
articles.trivia: The European Union is a political and economic union of 27 member states that are located primarily in Europe.

articles.headline: ISRO Successfully Conducts Advanced Test of Reusable Launch Vehicle Technology
articles.explainer:
• The Indian Space Research Organisation (ISRO) today announced the successful completion of a critical test for its Reusable Launch Vehicle (RLV) program.
• The test involved a precision landing experiment of the RLV-TD (Technology Demonstrator) at a designated runway, simulating operational conditions.
• This achievement marks a significant milestone in India's quest to develop fully reusable rocket technology, aiming to drastically reduce space launch costs.
• Reusable launch vehicles are crucial for future space exploration, satellite deployment, and potentially space tourism.
• The technology demonstrator is paving the way for a two-stage-to-orbit (TSTO) fully reusable launch vehicle.
gsSubTopics: Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life.
Achievements of Indians in science & technology; indigenization of technology and developing new technology.
Awareness in the fields of Space.
prelimsPoints: ISRO's RLV-TD program aims to develop technologies for a fully reusable launch vehicle.
Previous tests include hypersonic flight experiment (HEX) and landing experiment (LEX).
Reusable launch vehicles reduce the cost of access to space by allowing multiple uses of expensive rocket components.
Globally, companies like SpaceX (Falcon 9) and Blue Origin are pioneers in reusable rocket technology.
India's Gaganyaan mission for human spaceflight will also benefit from advancements in re-entry and landing technologies.
mainsPoints: Discuss the strategic importance of developing reusable launch vehicle technology for India's space program and its implications for global space commerce.
Analyze the economic and technological advantages of reusable rockets over traditional expendable launch vehicles.
Examine the challenges ISRO faces in perfecting RLV technology, including materials science, propulsion, and guidance systems.
Evaluate how advancements in RLV technology can boost India's capabilities in satellite launch, deep space missions, and future human spaceflight endeavors.
Suggest policy measures to support indigenous R&D and private sector participation in India's reusable space technology development.
articles.imageDescription: An artistic rendering of ISRO's Reusable Launch Vehicle (RLV) descending for a precision landing on a runway.
articles.trivia: The first fully reusable orbital spacecraft was NASA's Space Shuttle, which first flew in 1981.

articles.headline: Centre Unveils New Policy Framework for Urban Climate Resilience and Sustainable Cities
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Housing and Urban Affairs today launched a comprehensive policy framework aimed at enhancing urban climate resilience and promoting sustainable urban development across India.
• The policy focuses on integrating climate change adaptation and mitigation strategies into urban planning, infrastructure development, and service delivery.
• Key pillars include green building codes, efficient waste management, water conservation, sustainable transport, and nature-based solutions.
• Financial incentives and technical assistance will be provided to states and urban local bodies to implement the new guidelines.
• This initiative is critical for protecting urban populations from climate impacts such as heatwaves, floods, and water scarcity, while fostering livable cities.
gsSubTopics: Environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment.
Disaster and disaster management.
Infrastructure: Energy, Ports, Roads, Airports, Railways etc.
Urbanization, their problems and their remedies.
prelimsPoints: India is rapidly urbanizing, with over 35% of its population living in urban areas.
Urban areas are particularly vulnerable to climate change impacts due to high population density and critical infrastructure.
Smart Cities Mission (launched 2015) aims to promote sustainable and inclusive cities.
National Action Plan on Climate Change (NAPCC) includes missions like National Mission on Sustainable Habitat.
Nature-based solutions involve using natural processes and ecosystems to address societal challenges, including climate change.
mainsPoints: Analyze the multifaceted challenges posed by climate change to Indian urban centers and the necessity of a dedicated urban climate resilience policy.
Discuss the key features and potential impact of the new policy framework on urban governance, infrastructure development, and quality of life.
Examine the role of technology, citizen participation, and inter-agency coordination in achieving sustainable and climate-resilient cities.
Critically evaluate the financial and implementation hurdles in mainstreaming climate resilience into urban planning across diverse Indian cities.
Suggest innovative approaches and best practices from global examples that India can adopt for building future-proof urban environments.
articles.imageDescription: A modern cityscape with green spaces, renewable energy installations, and smart infrastructure, symbolizing sustainable urban development.
articles.trivia: Approximately 70% of global greenhouse gas emissions originate from urban areas.

prelimsSyllabus.subject: Environment
prelimsSyllabus.topic: Impact of Climate Change on India

articles.headline: India's Q2 GDP Growth Projections Remain Robust Amidst Global Headwinds
articles.explainer:
• Leading financial institutions and government economists released their projections for India's Q2 (July-September) GDP growth, indicating continued robust performance.
• Despite global economic slowdowns and geopolitical uncertainties, domestic demand and government capital expenditure are seen as key drivers.
• Manufacturing and services sectors are expected to show strong recovery, contributing significantly to the overall growth.
• Concerns remain regarding persistent inflation, though efforts by the RBI are expected to temper price rises in the coming quarters.
• The projections reinforce India's position as one of the fastest-growing major economies globally.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization, of resources, growth, development and employment.
Government Budgeting.

Major crops-cropping patterns in various parts of the country, - different types of irrigation and irrigation system storage, transport and marketing of agricultural produce and issues and related constraints; e-technology in the aid of farmers.
Effects of liberalization on the economy, changes in industrial policy and their effects on industrial growth.
prelimsPoints: GDP measures the total value of goods and services produced within a country's borders in a specific period.
India's fiscal year runs from April 1 to March 31. Q2 is July-September.
Key drivers of Indian economy: private consumption, investment, government expenditure, and net exports.
RBI uses monetary policy (e.g., repo rate) to control inflation.
The National Statistical Office (NSO) under MoSPI releases official GDP data.
mainsPoints: Analyze the factors contributing to India's robust GDP growth in the current global economic scenario.
Discuss the role of government capital expenditure and domestic consumption in sustaining economic momentum.
Examine the challenges posed by inflation, global geopolitical tensions, and supply chain disruptions to India's growth trajectory.
Evaluate the effectiveness of monetary and fiscal policies in achieving macroeconomic stability and fostering inclusive growth.
Suggest structural reforms and policy interventions needed to ensure long-term sustainable and equitable economic development for India.
articles.imageDescription: A bar graph showing upward trend of economic growth, with Indian currency symbols.
articles.trivia: India's economy is the fifth-largest in the world by nominal GDP and the third-largest by purchasing power parity (PPP).

articles.headline: Ministry of Home Affairs Boosts Border Security Modernization Along Northern Borders
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Home Affairs announced a significant modernization drive for border security forces deployed along India's northern borders.
• The initiative includes deployment of advanced surveillance technology, procurement of new-generation weaponry, and enhanced training for personnel.
• Focus areas are improving situational awareness, rapid response capabilities, and strengthening infrastructure in challenging terrains.
• The move is aimed at bolstering national security, countering infiltration, and addressing evolving threats from across the border.
• Integrated Border Management System (IBMS) is a key component, leveraging technology for comprehensive border control.
gsSubTopics: Security challenges and their management in border areas – linkages of organized crime with terrorism.
Various Security forces and agencies and their mandate.
prelimsPoints: India shares land borders with seven countries: Pakistan, China, Nepal, Bhutan, Bangladesh, Myanmar, and Afghanistan (PoK).
Border guarding forces: BSF (Pakistan, Bangladesh), ITBP (China), SSB (Nepal, Bhutan), Assam Rifles (Myanmar).
Integrated Border Management System (IBMS) aims for comprehensive management using technology.
Fencing, floodlights, surveillance cameras, thermal imagers, sensors are part of border security measures.
'Smart Fencing' or 'Comprehensive Integrated Border Management System (CIBMS)' projects are being implemented.
mainsPoints: Analyze the multi-faceted security challenges faced by India along its northern borders, including geopolitical dynamics and technological advancements.
Discuss the significance of modernizing border security forces and deploying advanced surveillance technologies in addressing these challenges.
Examine the concept of an Integrated Border Management System (IBMS) and its role in enhancing border effectiveness and reducing human intervention.
Evaluate the balance between security imperatives and the rights of border populations in the implementation of enhanced border security measures.
Suggest comprehensive strategies, including diplomatic, economic, and security measures, for effective border management in challenging terrains.
articles.imageDescription: Border Security Force (BSF) personnel patrolling a snowy mountainous terrain with modern equipment.
articles.trivia: The Border Security Force (BSF) is one of the world's largest border guarding forces.

prelimsSyllabus.subject: Internal Security
prelimsSyllabus.area: Security challenges and their management in border areas

articles.headline: Progress Report on National Education Policy 2020 Implementation in Higher Education
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Education released a detailed progress report on the implementation of the National Education Policy (NEP) 2020 in the higher education sector.
• The report highlights strides made in multidisciplinary education, academic bank of credits (ABC), and flexible learning pathways.
• Challenges remain in faculty recruitment, infrastructure development, and ensuring equitable access to quality education, especially in remote areas.
• The NEP aims to transform India's education system to meet the demands of the 21st century and foster holistic development.
• Emphasis is placed on research, innovation, and internationalization of higher education institutions.
gsSubTopics: Development processes and the development industry —the role of NGOs, SHGs, various groups and associations, donors, charities, institutional and other stakeholders.

Welfare schemes for vulnerable sections of the population by the Centre and States and the performance of these schemes; mechanisms, laws, institutions and Bodies constituted for the protection and betterment of these vulnerable sections.
prelimsPoints: NEP 2020 replaced the 1986 National Policy on Education.
Key features of NEP in higher education: 4-year undergraduate degrees with multiple exit options, ABC, MERUs, NRF.
Aim to increase Gross Enrolment Ratio (GER) in higher education to 50% by 2035.
Focus on multidisciplinary and holistic education, breaking silos between arts, science, and vocational streams.
UGC (University Grants Commission) and AICTE (All India Council for Technical Education) are key regulatory bodies.
mainsPoints: Analyze the transformative potential of the National Education Policy 2020 for India's higher education system.
Discuss the progress made and the challenges encountered in implementing key reforms such as multidisciplinary education and the Academic Bank of Credits.
Examine how NEP 2020 aims to address issues of quality, access, equity, and accountability in higher education.
Critically evaluate the role of faculty development, infrastructure, and funding in the successful realization of NEP's goals.
Suggest strategies to overcome implementation hurdles and ensure that NEP 2020 effectively prepares Indian youth for the future workforce.
articles.imageDescription: Students in a modern university classroom, engaged in collaborative learning, symbolizing multidisciplinary education.
articles.trivia: The Kothari Commission (1964-66) recommended the 6% of GDP expenditure on education, a target still aspired to by NEP 2020.

prelimsSyllabus.subject: Social Justice
prelimsSyllabus.area: Development and Management of Social Sector/Services

articles.headline: Inter-State Council Convenes to Address Critical Water Sharing Disputes
articles.explainer:
• The Inter-State Council convened a high-level meeting today to deliberate on long-standing water sharing disputes between several Indian states.
• Chaired by the Prime Minister, the meeting aimed to foster cooperative federalism and find amicable solutions through dialogue and consensus.
• Discussions focused on equitable distribution of river waters, mechanisms for dispute resolution, and efficient water resource management.
• The council emphasized the need for a national framework for inter-state river water management, considering climate change impacts.
• This meeting underscores the constitutional role of the Inter-State Council in promoting harmony between the Union and states.
gsSubTopics: Functions and responsibilities of the Union and the States, issues and challenges pertaining to the federal structure, devolution of powers and finances up to local levels and challenges therein.
Separation of powers between various organs dispute redressal mechanisms and institutions.
prelimsPoints: Inter-State Council is a constitutional body established under Article 263 of the Indian Constitution.
It is chaired by the Prime Minister and includes Chief Ministers of all states and UTs with legislative assemblies, and Union Ministers.
Its mandate is to investigate and discuss subjects of common interest between the Union and states, and make recommendations.
Inter-State Water Disputes Act, 1956, provides for adjudication of disputes relating to inter-state river waters.
River water disputes are often complex due to varying hydrological conditions, historical usage, and political factors.
mainsPoints: Analyze the constitutional provisions and institutional mechanisms for resolving inter-state water disputes in India, highlighting their effectiveness and limitations.
Discuss the socio-economic and political dimensions of water sharing disputes, particularly in the context of increasing water scarcity and climate change.
Examine the role of the Inter-State Council in fostering cooperative federalism and achieving consensus on contentious issues like river water management.
Critically evaluate the need for a comprehensive national framework for inter-state river water management that considers basin-level planning and integrated resource use.
Suggest reforms to the existing dispute resolution mechanisms to ensure timely and equitable solutions to inter-state water conflicts.
articles.imageDescription: A map of India overlaid with river systems, symbolizing inter-state water disputes.
articles.trivia: The Sarkaria Commission (1983) recommended the establishment of a permanent Inter-State Council.

articles.headline: India Hosts G20 Finance Ministers Meeting on Global Debt Relief and Sustainable Finance
articles.explainer:
• India today hosted a crucial G20 Finance Ministers and Central Bank Governors (FMCBG) meeting, focusing on addressing the escalating global debt crisis.
• Discussions centered on effective debt relief mechanisms for vulnerable low-income countries and promoting sustainable finance practices.
• India's presidency agenda emphasized collaborative solutions for global economic stability and resilient financial systems.
• The meeting explored innovative financing tools for climate action and sustainable development goals (SDGs).
• Outcomes are expected to contribute to a common framework for debt restructuring and enhanced multilateral cooperation.
gsSubTopics: Important International institutions, agencies and fora, their structure, mandate.
Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests.
prelimsPoints: G20 (Group of Twenty) is an intergovernmental forum comprising 19 countries and the European Union.
It works to address major issues related to the global economy, such as international financial stability, climate change mitigation, and sustainable development.
India held the G20 Presidency in 2023, setting precedents for future presidencies.
Global debt has reached record levels, exacerbated by the pandemic and rising interest rates.
Sustainable finance integrates environmental, social, and governance (ESG) considerations into financial decision-making.
mainsPoints: Analyze the causes and implications of the escalating global debt crisis, particularly for developing and low-income countries.
Discuss India's role and contributions as a G20 member in shaping global economic agenda, with a focus on debt relief and sustainable finance.
Examine the effectiveness of existing multilateral mechanisms and frameworks for debt restructuring and climate finance.
Evaluate the challenges in mobilizing adequate and affordable finance for achieving Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) in the global South.
Suggest policy recommendations for strengthening global financial architecture and promoting equitable and sustainable development.
articles.imageDescription: G20 logo prominently displayed on a podium, with world leaders or finance ministers in a conference setting.
articles.trivia: The G20 represents around two-thirds of the world's population, 85% of global GDP, and 75% of international trade.

prelimsSyllabus.subject: International Relations
prelimsSyllabus.area: Important International institutions, agencies and fora

articles.headline: Five New Indian Wetlands Designated as Ramsar Sites, Boosting Conservation Efforts
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change announced the addition of five new wetlands to the list of Ramsar Sites in India.
• These new designations elevate India's global standing in wetland conservation and biodiversity protection.
• The newly added sites represent diverse ecological characteristics, including unique freshwater and coastal ecosystems.
• This move will unlock international support and expertise for the conservation and wise use of these critical habitats.
• Wetlands play a vital role in water purification, flood control, climate regulation, and supporting unique flora and fauna.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment.
Biodiversity and Climate Change.
prelimsPoints: The Ramsar Convention (Convention on Wetlands) is an intergovernmental treaty for the conservation and wise use of wetlands.
It was signed in Ramsar, Iran, in 1971 and came into force in 1975.
A 'Ramsar Site' is a wetland designated to be of international importance under the Convention.
India has a significant number of Ramsar sites, reflecting its rich wetland biodiversity.
Wetlands are crucial for migratory birds, fish breeding, and maintaining hydrological cycles.
mainsPoints: Analyze the ecological and socio-economic significance of wetlands in India, and the threats they face from anthropogenic activities and climate change.
Discuss the objectives and impact of the Ramsar Convention on wetland conservation efforts in India.
Examine the challenges in effective management and sustainable utilization of Ramsar Sites, including community participation and funding.
Evaluate how the designation of new Ramsar Sites contributes to India's national and international commitments towards biodiversity conservation.
Suggest comprehensive strategies for wetland protection, including policy interventions, public awareness, and inter-sectoral coordination.
articles.imageDescription: A serene wetland landscape with migratory birds and lush vegetation, highlighting biodiversity.
articles.trivia: Chilika Lake in Odisha was the first Indian wetland of international importance designated under the Ramsar Convention.

articles.headline: NITI Aayog Releases Ethical Guidelines for AI Deployment in Healthcare
articles.explainer:
• NITI Aayog today unveiled a comprehensive set of ethical guidelines for the responsible and equitable deployment of Artificial Intelligence (AI) in the healthcare sector.
• The guidelines address critical concerns such as data privacy, algorithmic bias, patient safety, transparency, and accountability in AI-powered diagnostics and treatment.
• The framework aims to foster innovation while ensuring ethical considerations are paramount in the rapidly evolving field of AI in health.
• It recommends robust regulatory oversight, independent audits, and continuous monitoring of AI systems in clinical settings.
• These guidelines are crucial for building trust in AI technologies and ensuring they benefit all sections of society.
gsSubTopics: Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life.
Achievements of Indians in science & technology; indigenization of technology and developing new technology.
Awareness in the fields of IT, Space, Computers, robotics, nano-technology, bio-technology and issues relating to intellectual property rights.
prelimsPoints: NITI Aayog is the premier policy 'think tank' of the Government of India.
AI in healthcare applications include diagnostics (e.g., radiology, pathology), drug discovery, personalized medicine, and robotic surgery.
Algorithmic bias occurs when an algorithm produces results that are systematically prejudiced.
India's National Digital Health Mission aims to create a digital health ecosystem.
Ethical AI frameworks are being developed globally to ensure responsible AI development and deployment.
mainsPoints: Analyze the transformative potential of Artificial Intelligence in revolutionizing healthcare delivery and patient outcomes in India.
Discuss the ethical challenges and risks associated with deploying AI in sensitive domains like healthcare, focusing on data privacy, bias, and accountability.
Examine the key features of NITI Aayog's ethical guidelines and their significance in fostering responsible AI innovation in India.
Critically evaluate the regulatory and governance frameworks required to ensure equitable access and prevent misuse of AI technologies in healthcare.
Suggest a multi-stakeholder approach involving government, industry, academia, and civil society to build a robust and ethical AI ecosystem in Indian healthcare.
articles.imageDescription: A doctor interacting with a holographic patient interface, symbolizing AI in healthcare, with data security icons.
articles.trivia: The term 'Artificial Intelligence' was coined by John McCarthy in 1956.

articles.headline: MSME Sector Records Robust Growth Driven by 'Make in India' Initiatives
articles.explainer:
• The Micro, Small, and Medium Enterprises (MSME) sector has shown robust growth in the past year, largely attributed to supportive government policies under the 'Make in India' initiative.
• Increased access to credit, simplified regulatory compliance, and enhanced market linkages have fueled the expansion of MSMEs.
• The sector's contribution to employment generation and exports has seen a significant uptick, bolstering economic resilience.
• Government procurement policies favoring MSMEs and digital platforms like GeM (Government e-Marketplace) have played a crucial role.
• This growth is vital for achieving inclusive economic development and strengthening India's manufacturing base.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization, of resources, growth, development and employment.
Inclusive growth and issues arising from it.
Effects of liberalization on the economy, changes in industrial policy and their effects on industrial growth.
prelimsPoints: MSMEs are defined based on investment in plant & machinery/equipment and turnover.
MSMEs are crucial for employment generation, especially in rural and semi-urban areas.
'Make in India' initiative (launched 2014) aims to boost domestic manufacturing.
GeM is an online platform for public procurement of goods and services.
The Emergency Credit Line Guarantee Scheme (ECLGS) was introduced during the pandemic to support MSMEs.
mainsPoints: Analyze the significance of the MSME sector for India's economic growth, employment generation, and inclusive development.
Discuss the various government initiatives under 'Make in India' and their impact on fostering the growth and competitiveness of MSMEs.
Examine the challenges faced by MSMEs in India, including access to credit, technology adoption, market access, and skilled labor.
Critically evaluate the role of digital platforms and policy reforms in empowering MSMEs and integrating them into global value chains.
Suggest further policy measures and reforms to unlock the full potential of the MSME sector and ensure its sustainable growth.
articles.imageDescription: A factory floor with modern machinery, symbolizing manufacturing and MSME growth.
articles.trivia: MSMEs contribute approximately 30% to India's GDP and over 45% to its total exports.

articles.headline: Electoral Reforms Committee Submits Report on Digital Voting and Remote Participation
articles.explainer:
• A high-level committee on electoral reforms submitted its comprehensive report to the Election Commission of India, focusing on the feasibility and security of digital voting.
• The report explores various models for remote voting for migrant workers and overseas citizens, leveraging secure technological solutions.
• Recommendations include phased implementation, robust cybersecurity protocols, and ensuring transparency and verifiability of digital ballots.
• The committee also addressed concerns regarding voter privacy, potential for manipulation, and public trust in electronic voting systems.
• This initiative aims to enhance voter participation and make the electoral process more accessible in the digital age.
gsSubTopics: Salient features of the Representation of People’s Act.
Appointment to various Constitutional posts, powers, functions and responsibilities of various Constitutional Bodies.
E-governance- applications, models, successes, limitations, and potential; citizens charters, transparency & accountability and institutional and other measures.
prelimsPoints: Election Commission of India (ECI) is an autonomous constitutional authority responsible for administering election processes in India.
Article 324 of the Constitution vests the power of superintendence, direction, and control of elections in the ECI.
EVMs have been used in Indian elections since 1982, with VVPATs introduced to enhance transparency and verifiability.
Remote voting aims to allow citizens to cast their votes from a constituency other than their registered one.
Challenges include ensuring secrecy, preventing coercion, and verifying voter identity remotely.
mainsPoints: Analyze the imperative for electoral reforms in India, particularly focusing on enhancing voter participation and addressing issues of accessibility.
Discuss the potential benefits and inherent risks of introducing digital voting and remote participation mechanisms in India's electoral system.
Examine the technological, legal, and logistical challenges in implementing secure and transparent digital voting, including cybersecurity and voter authentication.
Critically evaluate the implications of such reforms on voter trust, democratic principles, and the overall integrity of the electoral process.
Suggest a roadmap for phased implementation of digital voting, emphasizing pilot projects, public awareness, and robust legislative safeguards.
articles.imageDescription: A hand casting a vote on a digital screen, with a biometric scanner, symbolizing digital voting.
articles.trivia: India has the largest electorate in the world, with over 900 million registered voters.

articles.headline: National Health Mission Intensifies Focus on Non-Communicable Diseases (NCDs)
articles.explainer:
• The National Health Mission (NHM) announced an intensified focus on prevention, control, and management of Non-Communicable Diseases (NCDs) across India.
• The new strategy involves strengthening primary healthcare infrastructure, promoting healthy lifestyles, and early screening for NCDs like diabetes, hypertension, and cancer.
• Special emphasis will be placed on digital health solutions, community engagement, and capacity building for healthcare professionals.
• NCDs are a growing public health challenge, accounting for a significant burden of disease and mortality in India.
• This initiative aims to reduce premature mortality from NCDs and improve the overall health outcomes of the population.
gsSubTopics: Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health, Education, Human Resources.

Welfare schemes for vulnerable sections of the population by the Centre and States and the performance of these schemes; mechanisms, laws, institutions and Bodies constituted for the protection and betterment of these vulnerable sections.
articles.source: Ministry of Health and Family Welfare
prelimsPoints: National Health Mission (NHM) was launched in 2013 by merging National Rural Health Mission (NRHM) and National Urban Health Mission (NUHM).
NCDs include cardiovascular diseases, cancers, chronic respiratory diseases, and diabetes.
Risk factors for NCDs include unhealthy diet, physical inactivity, tobacco use, and harmful use of alcohol.
Ayushman Bharat Yojana aims to provide health insurance coverage and establish Health and Wellness Centres.
India has committed to reducing premature mortality from NCDs by one-third by 2030 as part of SDG 3.4.
mainsPoints: Analyze the growing burden of Non-Communicable Diseases (NCDs) in India and their socio-economic implications.
Discuss the strategies and interventions adopted by the National Health Mission to address the challenge of NCDs, focusing on prevention and early detection.
Examine the role of primary healthcare, digital health technologies, and community participation in strengthening NCD control programs.
Critically evaluate the challenges in implementing NCD prevention and management strategies, including resource constraints and behavioral factors.
Suggest a multi-sectoral approach involving public health, education, and other sectors to combat NCDs effectively and promote a healthier population.
articles.imageDescription: A healthcare professional conducting a health check-up, with infographics on NCD prevention.
articles.trivia: Globally, NCDs kill 41 million people each year, equivalent to 74% of all deaths.

prelimsSyllabus.subject: Social Justice
prelimsSyllabus.area: Development and Management of Social Sector/Services

articles.headline: Editorial: India's Demographic Dividend - Harnessing Potential Amidst Evolving Challenges
articles.explainer:
• Today's editorial discusses India's unique position of having the world's largest youth population, often termed its 'demographic dividend'.
• It highlights the immense potential for economic growth and innovation if this young workforce is adequately skilled, educated, and employed.
• The editorial also cautions about the 'demographic disaster' risk if challenges like unemployment, skill gaps, and inadequate social infrastructure are not addressed.
• It calls for urgent policy interventions in education, healthcare, and job creation to fully capitalize on this demographic window.
• The piece emphasizes the regional disparities in demographic transition and the need for tailored strategies.
gsSubTopics: Population and associated issues, poverty and developmental issues, urbanization, their problems and their remedies.
Salient features of Indian Society, Diversity of India.
prelimsPoints: Demographic dividend refers to the economic growth potential that can result from shifts in a population’s age structure.
It occurs when the share of the working-age population (15-64 years) is larger than the non-working-age share.
India's Total Fertility Rate (TFR) has fallen below the replacement level of 2.1, indicating a demographic shift.
States like Uttar Pradesh and Bihar are still in earlier stages of demographic transition compared to southern states.
Challenges include ensuring quality education, vocational training, healthcare, and creation of sufficient jobs.
mainsPoints: Analyze the concept of demographic dividend and its implications for India's economic growth and social development.
Discuss the opportunities presented by India's large youth population and the sectors that can benefit most from this demographic shift.
Examine the challenges India faces in harnessing its demographic dividend, including issues of education, skill development, employment, and healthcare.
Critically evaluate the role of government policies and schemes in addressing these challenges and ensuring inclusive development for the youth.
Suggest a comprehensive strategy that integrates education, health, and economic policies to convert India's demographic dividend into a sustainable economic advantage.
articles.imageDescription: A diverse group of young Indian professionals, symbolizing the potential of the demographic dividend.
articles.trivia: India surpassed China as the world's most populous country in 2023.

articles.headline: INS Vikrant Successfully Completes Critical Aviation Trials, Nearing Full Operational Capability
articles.explainer:
• India's first indigenously built aircraft carrier, INS Vikrant, today successfully completed a series of critical aviation facility complex trials.
• The trials involved integrated operations of various aircraft types, including MiG-29K fighter jets and helicopters, demonstrating the carrier's full operational readiness.
• This achievement is a major milestone for the Indian Navy and India's 'Atmanirbhar Bharat' (self-reliant India) initiative in defence manufacturing.
• INS Vikrant significantly enhances India's naval power projection capabilities and maritime security in the Indo-Pacific region.
• The carrier is expected to be fully operational with its air wing in the coming months, bolstering India's blue-water navy ambitions.
gsSubTopics: Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life.
Achievements of Indians in science & technology; indigenization of technology and developing new technology.
Security challenges and their management in border areas – linkages of organized crime with terrorism.
prelimsPoints: INS Vikrant is India's first indigenously designed and built aircraft carrier.
It was commissioned into the Indian Navy in September 2022.
The carrier is capable of operating a diverse air wing, including MiG-29K fighter jets, Kamov-31, MH-60R multi-role helicopters, and ALH.
It employs a Short Take-Off But Arrested Recovery (STOBAR) system for aircraft launch and recovery.
India is among a select few nations with the capability to design and build an aircraft carrier.
mainsPoints: Analyze the strategic significance of INS Vikrant for India's naval capabilities and its role in projecting power in the Indo-Pacific region.
Discuss how the indigenous construction of INS Vikrant aligns with the 'Atmanirbhar Bharat' initiative and boosts India's defence manufacturing ecosystem.
Examine the technological complexities and challenges involved in designing, building, and operating an aircraft carrier.
Evaluate the implications of a stronger Indian blue-water navy for regional security dynamics and India's geopolitical interests.
Suggest future steps for India's naval modernization, including the development of future aircraft carriers and advanced naval aviation technologies.
articles.imageDescription: INS Vikrant sailing in the open sea with fighter jets taking off and landing on its deck.
articles.trivia: The name 'Vikrant' means 'courageous' in Sanskrit.

articles.headline: New Policy to Expedite Solar Energy Integration into National Grid
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of New and Renewable Energy unveiled a new policy framework aimed at expediting the integration of large-scale solar energy projects into the national grid.
• The policy addresses bottlenecks in land acquisition, transmission infrastructure, and regulatory approvals for solar power developers.
• It introduces incentives for hybrid renewable energy projects and energy storage solutions to ensure grid stability.
• The move is critical for India to achieve its ambitious renewable energy targets and enhance energy security.
• Experts laud the policy for its focus on streamlining processes and attracting further investment in the solar sector.
gsSubTopics: Infrastructure: Energy
Investment models
Environmental pollution and degradation
Conservation
Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life
prelimsPoints: India has set a target of achieving 500 GW of non-fossil fuel energy capacity by 2030.
Solar energy is a major component of India's renewable energy mix.
Challenges in grid integration include intermittency of solar power and need for balancing mechanisms.
Energy storage solutions (e.g., battery storage) are crucial for stabilizing the grid with high renewable penetration.
The Green Energy Corridor project aims to facilitate the evacuation of renewable energy.
mainsPoints: Analyze the critical role of solar energy in India's energy transition and achieving its climate change commitments.
Discuss the major challenges in integrating large-scale intermittent renewable energy sources like solar into the national grid.
Examine the key provisions of the new policy framework and how it aims to address existing bottlenecks in solar energy deployment.
Evaluate the importance of energy storage solutions and hybrid renewable energy projects in ensuring grid stability and reliability.
Suggest further policy and technological innovations needed to accelerate solar energy adoption and strengthen India's renewable energy infrastructure.
articles.imageDescription: A vast solar farm connected to a power grid, symbolizing renewable energy integration.
articles.trivia: India's Bhadla Solar Park in Rajasthan is one of the largest solar power plants in the world.

articles.headline: India Establishes National Quantum Computing Research Centre
articles.explainer:
• India today announced the establishment of the National Quantum Computing Research Centre (NQCRC), a major boost to its strategic technology capabilities.
• The centre will focus on cutting-edge research in quantum computing, quantum communication, and quantum sensing technologies.
• It aims to foster collaboration between academia, industry, and government to develop indigenous quantum technologies.
• Quantum computing has the potential to revolutionize fields like medicine, materials science, and cryptography.
• This initiative is part of India's National Quantum Mission, positioning the country as a leader in emerging technologies.
gsSubTopics: Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life.
Achievements of Indians in science & technology; indigenization of technology and developing new technology.
prelimsPoints: National Quantum Mission (NQM) was approved in 2023 with a budget of ₹6,000 crore.
Quantum computing uses principles of quantum mechanics (superposition, entanglement) to solve complex problems faster than classical computers.
Quantum communication enables ultra-secure communication through quantum cryptography.
Quantum sensing offers highly precise measurements for navigation, medical imaging, etc.
India aims to develop quantum technologies and applications by 2030.
mainsPoints: Analyze the transformative potential of quantum technologies (computing, communication, sensing) and their strategic importance for India.
Discuss the objectives and expected outcomes of the National Quantum Computing Research Centre (NQCRC) in advancing India's quantum capabilities.
Examine the challenges in developing indigenous quantum technologies, including talent acquisition, infrastructure, and funding.
Evaluate the ethical and security implications of quantum advancements, particularly in areas like cryptography.
Suggest a roadmap for India to become a global leader in quantum technologies, fostering a robust ecosystem of research, industry, and startups.
articles.imageDescription: A futuristic depiction of quantum particles interacting in a complex pattern, symbolizing quantum computing.
articles.trivia: A 'qubit' is the basic unit of quantum information, analogous to the 'bit' in classical computing.

articles.headline: India-Pakistan Hold Annual Dialogue on Indus Waters Treaty Amidst Regional Tensions
articles.explainer:
• Despite prevailing regional tensions, India and Pakistan successfully concluded their annual Permanent Indus Commission meeting today.
• The dialogue focused on technical issues related to water sharing, data exchange, and inspection of projects under the Indus Waters Treaty (IWT).
• Both sides reiterated their commitment to upholding the provisions of the treaty, which has survived multiple conflicts.
• The IWT is considered one of the most successful water-sharing agreements globally, facilitating cooperation despite political differences.
• Discussions also touched upon the impact of climate change on river flows and future water management strategies.
gsSubTopics: Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests.
Effect of policies and politics of developed and developing countries on India’s interests, Indian diaspora.
prelimsPoints: Indus Waters Treaty (IWT) was signed in 1960 between India and Pakistan, brokered by the World Bank.
It allocates the waters of the Eastern Rivers (Ravi, Beas, Sutlej) to India for unrestricted use.
It allocates the waters of the Western Rivers (Indus, Jhelum, Chenab) to Pakistan for unrestricted use.
India is allowed to use Western River waters for limited non-consumptive purposes like hydropower generation (run-of-the-river projects).
The Permanent Indus Commission (PIC) meets annually to implement the treaty.
mainsPoints: Analyze the historical context and salient features of the Indus Waters Treaty (IWT), highlighting its significance as a model for transboundary water management.
Discuss the challenges and complexities in maintaining the IWT amidst fluctuating India-Pakistan relations and regional geopolitical dynamics.
Examine the implications of climate change on the Indus Basin rivers and the future sustainability of the treaty's provisions.
Critically evaluate India's approach to the IWT, balancing its water security needs with its international commitments and regional stability.
Suggest measures to enhance cooperation and address emerging issues in the Indus Basin, promoting a more integrated water resource management approach.
articles.imageDescription: A map of the Indus River Basin, showing the flow of rivers through India and Pakistan.
articles.trivia: The Indus Waters Treaty is often cited as an example of successful conflict resolution and cooperation between two hostile nations.

articles.headline: Archaeologists Unearth Evidence of Large Harappan Settlement in Gujarat
articles.explainer:
• Archaeologists in Gujarat today announced the discovery of a significant new Harappan settlement, providing fresh insights into the ancient Indus Valley Civilization.
• The site, believed to be a major urban center, features well-preserved residential structures, pottery, and unique artifacts.
• Early findings suggest advanced town planning, sophisticated drainage systems, and evidence of extensive trade networks.
• This discovery could redefine the understanding of the geographical spread and cultural diversity of the Harappan civilization.
• Further excavations are expected to shed light on the socio-economic life and decline of this ancient civilization.
gsSubTopics: Indian culture will cover the salient aspects of Art Forms, Literature and Architecture from ancient to modern times.
History of India and Indian National Movement.
prelimsPoints: Indus Valley Civilization (IVC) existed from 2500 BCE to 1900 BCE.
Key sites include Harappa, Mohenjo-daro, Dholavira, Lothal, Rakhigarhi, Kalibangan.
Known for sophisticated urban planning, brick houses, elaborate drainage systems, and a unique script.
Lothal and Dholavira are prominent Harappan sites in Gujarat.
The decline of the IVC is attributed to various factors, including climate change, shifting river courses, and invasions.
mainsPoints: Analyze the distinct features of the Harappan civilization's urban planning, drainage systems, and socio-economic organization.
Discuss how recent archaeological discoveries, like the new settlement in Gujarat, contribute to our understanding of the geographical extent and cultural nuances of the Indus Valley Civilization.
Examine the evidence for trade and cultural contacts between the Harappan civilization and contemporary civilizations.
Critically evaluate the various theories regarding the decline of the Harappan civilization and the lessons that can be drawn from its collapse.
Suggest measures for the preservation and promotion of India's archaeological heritage, especially in the context of new discoveries.
articles.imageDescription: Archaeologists at an excavation site, carefully unearthing pottery and ancient structures.
articles.trivia: The Harappan civilization was one of the three early civilizations of the Near East and South Asia, along with the ancient Egyptian and Mesopotamian civilizations.

prelimsMocks.question:
With reference to the National Green Hydrogen Mission, consider the following statements:
1. It aims to achieve 5 Million Metric Tonnes (MMT) of green hydrogen production capacity by 2030.
2. The Strategic Interventions for Green Hydrogen Transition (SIGHT) programme is a component of this mission.
3. The mission targets a reduction in fossil fuel imports by over ₹1 lakh crore annually.

Which of the statements given above are correct?
prelimsMocks.explanation: All three statements are correct. The National Green Hydrogen Mission indeed aims for 5 MMT production capacity by 2030, includes the SIGHT programme, and targets a significant reduction in fossil fuel imports.

prelimsMocks.question: The 'Right to be Forgotten' in India is primarily derived from which of the following constitutional provisions?
options: (b) Article 19 (Right to Freedom of Speech and Expression)
(c) Article 21 (Right to Life and Personal Liberty)
prelimsMocks.answer: (c) Article 21 (Right to Life and Personal Liberty)
prelimsMocks.explanation: The 'Right to be Forgotten' is considered an integral part of the broader 'right to privacy', which has been recognized as a fundamental right under Article 21 of the Indian Constitution by the Supreme Court in the Puttaswamy judgment.

prelimsMocks.question:
Consider the following statements regarding the Inter-State Council:
1. It is a statutory body established under the Inter-State Water Disputes Act, 1956.
2. The Prime Minister serves as its ex-officio Chairman.
3. Its recommendations are binding on both the Union and State governments.

Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
prelimsMocks.explanation: Statement 1 is incorrect; the Inter-State Council is a constitutional body established under Article 263. Statement 3 is incorrect; its recommendations are advisory, not binding. Statement 2 is correct; the Prime Minister is indeed its ex-officio Chairman.

prelimsMocks.question:
Which of the following statements about India's first indigenously built aircraft carrier, INS Vikrant, is/are correct?
1. It was commissioned into the Indian Navy in 2022.
2. It employs a Catapult Assisted Take-Off But Arrested Recovery (CATOBAR) system.
3. It is designed to operate MiG-29K fighter jets and various helicopters.

Select the correct answer using the code given below:
prelimsMocks.explanation: Statement 1 is correct (commissioned in Sept 2022). Statement 2 is incorrect; INS Vikrant uses a Short Take-Off But Arrested Recovery (STOBAR) system, not CATOBAR. Statement 3 is correct (operates MiG-29K, Kamov-31, MH-60R, ALH).

prelimsMocks.question: The Indus Waters Treaty (IWT), signed between India and Pakistan, primarily allocates the waters of which of the following rivers to Pakistan for unrestricted use?
prelimsMocks.explanation: Under the Indus Waters Treaty, the waters of the Eastern Rivers (Ravi, Beas, Sutlej) are allocated to India, while the waters of the Western Rivers (Indus, Jhelum, Chenab) are allocated to Pakistan for unrestricted use.

mainsMocks.question: India's ambition to become a global leader in Green Hydrogen production faces significant technological and economic hurdles. Critically examine these challenges and suggest a comprehensive strategy to overcome them, ensuring a robust green hydrogen ecosystem.
mainsMocks.syllabusMatch: GS3: Infrastructure: Energy; Environmental pollution and degradation, conservation; Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life.
mainsMocks.approach: Introduce the National Green Hydrogen Mission and its importance. Discuss technological challenges (electrolyser efficiency, cost, storage, transport). Examine economic hurdles (high production cost, lack of demand, infrastructure investment). Propose solutions: R&D funding, PLI schemes, international collaboration, market creation, regulatory support, skill development. Conclude on strategic benefits.

mainsMocks.question: The 'Right to be Forgotten' represents a complex interplay between individual privacy, public interest, and freedom of expression in the digital age. Analyze the implications of the Supreme Court's recent verdict on this right for India's data governance framework and democratic discourse.
mainsMocks.syllabusMatch: GS2: Indian Constitution—fundamental rights; Government policies and interventions; Important aspects of governance, transparency and accountability.
mainsMocks.approach: Define 'Right to be Forgotten' and its constitutional basis (Art 21, Puttaswamy). Discuss its significance for individual privacy in the digital era. Analyze the inherent conflict with freedom of speech (Art 19) and public right to know. Examine the SC verdict's impact on data protection laws, responsibilities of data fiduciaries, and digital media. Conclude on the need for a balanced approach and robust implementation.

mainsMocks.question: Despite India's significant demographic dividend, challenges in education, skill development, and employment threaten to turn this advantage into a demographic burden. Discuss the critical issues hindering the realization of India's demographic potential and recommend policy measures to effectively harness it.
mainsMocks.syllabusMatch: GS1: Population and associated issues, poverty and developmental issues; Salient features of Indian Society. GS2: Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health, Education, Human Resources.
mainsMocks.approach: Explain demographic dividend and India's current position. Outline challenges: quality of education, skill-job mismatch, unemployment (especially youth/educated), health issues, gender disparity, regional variations. Propose policy measures: NEP 2020 effective implementation, vocational training, industry-academia linkage, entrepreneurship promotion, ease of doing business, investment in health, women's empowerment. Conclude with a vision for inclusive and sustainable growth.

GS2.headline: Supreme Court Delivers Landmark Verdict on 'Right to be Forgotten' in Digital Age
GS2.explainer:
• The Supreme Court today delivered a significant judgment clarifying the scope and applicability of the 'Right to be Forgotten' in India, especially concerning online personal data.
• The ruling emphasizes a delicate balance between an individual's right to privacy and the public's right to information, alongside freedom of speech and expression.
• It mandates specific conditions under which personal information can be de-indexed or removed from public search results and online platforms.
• The verdict provides much-needed clarity for data fiduciaries and individuals regarding data protection and digital rights.
• Experts suggest the ruling will significantly influence future data protection laws and digital governance frameworks in India.
gsSubTopics: Indian Constitution—historical underpinnings, evolution, features, amendments, significant provisions and basic structure.
Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation.

Important aspects of governance, transparency and accountability, e-governance- applications, models, successes, limitations, and potential; citizens charters, transparency & accountability and institutional and other measures.
prelimsPoints: Right to be Forgotten is part of the broader 'right to privacy' under Article 21 of the Indian Constitution.
Puttaswamy v. Union of India (2017) judgment recognized privacy as a fundamental right.
The Personal Data Protection Bill (now Act) has provisions related to the 'right to erasure' or 'right to be forgotten'.
Globally, GDPR (General Data Protection Regulation) in the EU recognizes this right.
It involves removal of personal information from public visibility, not necessarily permanent deletion from databases.
mainsPoints: Analyze the constitutional basis and jurisprudential evolution of the 'Right to be Forgotten' in India.
Discuss the challenges in implementing the 'Right to be Forgotten' in the context of digital platforms and global internet architecture.
Examine the potential conflict between the 'Right to be Forgotten' and other fundamental rights such as freedom of speech and the right to information.
Critically evaluate the implications of this Supreme Court verdict on data governance, individual privacy, and media freedom in India.
Suggest a balanced approach to uphold privacy rights while ensuring transparency and accountability in the digital sphere.
GS2.imageDescription: A stylized image showing a lock icon over digital data, representing data privacy and the 'right to be forgotten'.
GS2.trivia: The 'Right to be Forgotten' gained prominence after a 2014 European Court of Justice ruling against Google.

GS2.headline: India-EU Free Trade Agreement Negotiations Show Positive Momentum
GS2.explainer:
• India and the European Union concluded another round of Free Trade Agreement (FTA) negotiations, reporting significant progress on several key chapters.
• Both sides expressed optimism about reaching a comprehensive and mutually beneficial agreement in the near future.
• Key areas of discussion included market access for goods and services, investment protection, intellectual property rights, and geographical indications.
• An FTA is expected to boost bilateral trade and investment, creating new opportunities for businesses in both regions.
• The agreement aims to reduce tariffs and non-tariff barriers, fostering deeper economic integration between India and the EU.
gsSubTopics: Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests.
Effect of policies and politics of developed and developing countries on India’s interests, Indian diaspora.
prelimsPoints: India-EU FTA negotiations resumed in 2022 after an eight-year hiatus.
The EU is one of India's largest trading partners.
Key Indian exports to EU: engineering goods, pharmaceuticals, chemicals, textiles, agricultural products.
Key EU exports to India: machinery, transport equipment, chemicals, diamonds.
India is also negotiating FTAs with countries like the UK, Canada, and Australia.
mainsPoints: Analyze the strategic importance of a comprehensive FTA between India and the European Union for India's economic growth and global positioning.
Discuss the potential benefits and challenges for various sectors of the Indian economy from an India-EU FTA.
Examine how the FTA could impact India's 'Make in India' initiative and its integration into global supply chains.
Evaluate the role of non-tariff barriers and regulatory convergence in the success of the India-EU FTA.
Suggest measures India should take to maximize gains and mitigate risks from such a trade agreement.
GS2.imageDescription: Flags of India and the European Union crossed, symbolizing trade and cooperation.
GS2.trivia: The European Union is a political and economic union of 27 member states that are located primarily in Europe.

GS2.headline: Progress Report on National Education Policy 2020 Implementation in Higher Education
GS2.explainer:
• The Ministry of Education released a detailed progress report on the implementation of the National Education Policy (NEP) 2020 in the higher education sector.
• The report highlights strides made in multidisciplinary education, academic bank of credits (ABC), and flexible learning pathways.
• Challenges remain in faculty recruitment, infrastructure development, and ensuring equitable access to quality education, especially in remote areas.
• The NEP aims to transform India's education system to meet the demands of the 21st century and foster holistic development.
• Emphasis is placed on research, innovation, and internationalization of higher education institutions.
gsSubTopics: Development processes and the development industry —the role of NGOs, SHGs, various groups and associations, donors, charities, institutional and other stakeholders.

Welfare schemes for vulnerable sections of the population by the Centre and States and the performance of these schemes; mechanisms, laws, institutions and Bodies constituted for the protection and betterment of these vulnerable sections.
prelimsPoints: NEP 2020 replaced the 1986 National Policy on Education.
Key features of NEP in higher education: 4-year undergraduate degrees with multiple exit options, ABC, MERUs, NRF.
Aim to increase Gross Enrolment Ratio (GER) in higher education to 50% by 2035.
Focus on multidisciplinary and holistic education, breaking silos between arts, science, and vocational streams.
UGC (University Grants Commission) and AICTE (All India Council for Technical Education) are key regulatory bodies.
mainsPoints: Analyze the transformative potential of the National Education Policy 2020 for India's higher education system.
Discuss the progress made and the challenges encountered in implementing key reforms such as multidisciplinary education and the Academic Bank of Credits.
Examine how NEP 2020 aims to address issues of quality, access, equity, and accountability in higher education.
Critically evaluate the role of faculty development, infrastructure, and funding in the successful realization of NEP's goals.
Suggest strategies to overcome implementation hurdles and ensure that NEP 2020 effectively prepares Indian youth for the future workforce.
GS2.imageDescription: Students in a modern university classroom, engaged in collaborative learning, symbolizing multidisciplinary education.
GS2.trivia: The Kothari Commission (1964-66) recommended the 6% of GDP expenditure on education, a target still aspired to by NEP 2020.
